coaching.coaching: State the concrete attestation evidence the [operator-attested] pre-sign pin recheck semantics at signing time will require (a change id / vote outcome / timestamp), recorded on the ticket.
coaching.subject: pre-sign pin recheck semantics at signing time

coaching.coaching: Prove scope boundary for a single-session execution slice end-to-end with a thin vertical slice first.
coaching.subject: scope boundary for a single-session execution slice

coaching.coaching: Plan how acceptance-criteria completeness and testability will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: acceptance-criteria completeness and testability

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for unflagged execution-mode blocking-floor rollout decision — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: unflagged execution-mode blocking-floor rollout decision

coaching.coaching: Propagate the revision for missing dependency between phase-compatibility child tickets to the child tickets.
coaching.subject: missing dependency between phase-compatibility child tickets

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the Settled Design whether pin collection runs before LLM prerequisite evaluation, and confirm that an unresolvable prerequisite always prevents signing (pin collection rule takes precedence), so the indeterminate-cannot-block rule only applies when the target is readable but evaluation fails.
evidence: 'Pin collection is all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets prevent signing.'
'indeterminate records why evaluation or attribution could not be completed and cannot block'

Both rules apply when a prerequisite ticket is unresolvable, but the plan does not state whether pin collection runs before LLM evaluation, nor whether an unresolvable prerequisite always forecloses the indeterminate path.
findings.finding: An unresolvable prerequisite ticket triggers two contradictory outcomes across sections: pin collection (all-or-nothing) prevents signing entirely, while the prerequisite consistency review guarantees that an indeterminate record 'cannot block'. If both mechanisms apply to the same unresolvable ticket, an implementer faces contradictory direction — is signing blocked (pin collection rule) or is the outcome merely indeterminate and non-blocking (prerequisite review rule)?
findings.location: Settled design — 'Signed plan-material pins' section vs. 'Prerequisite consistency review' section
scenarios: Implementer A treats unresolvable prerequisite as fatal to signing (following pin collection rule). Implementer B allows signing with an indeterminate record (following prerequisite review rule). The two implementations contradict each other on a well-defined input.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Signed plan-material pins' section: 'Pin collection is attempted before LLM evaluation; an unresolvable target causes signing to fail regardless of the prerequisite consistency review outcome. The indeterminate disposition applies only when the prerequisite ticket is readable but LLM evaluation or attribution cannot be completed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the pre-sign recheck re-collects all relationship pins afresh (not re-validates the snapshot's cached values), and document what happens when the freshly-collected pin set differs from the snapshot (treat as material drift → abort signing).
evidence: Plan text: 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing.'

Current sign_plan_review() in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py lines 74-136 computes material and deps at sign time with no pre-LLM snapshot checkpoint — the snapshot/recheck pattern is a new mechanism, not an extension of an existing one.

If 'recheck' only re-validates the previously-collected pin values (rather than re-collecting them), a newly-added depends_on link between snapshot and sign would produce a pin-incomplete attestation, violating 'Pin coverage includes every normalized direct prerequisite' (AC).
ABSENCE: the plan does not state that pins are re-collected at sign time vs. re-verified from the snapshot.
findings.finding: The pre-sign recheck semantics for pins are ambiguous about whether pins are re-collected afresh at sign time or merely snapshot-validated. The current sign_plan_review() computes all manifest inputs (material, deps) at signing time — there is no pre-LLM snapshot that is later re-verified. The plan introduces a new 'snapshot before LLM + recheck before sign' contract specifically for pins, but leaves unspecified whether 'recheck' re-collects every related ticket's fingerprint or only re-checks the previously-collected set. If a new prerequisite is added (or an existing one materially changes) between the pre-LLM snapshot and the pre-sign recheck, and the subject ticket's own material fingerprint is unaffected, the mechanism could sign an attestation with a stale or incomplete pin set — defeating the goal of binding 'exactly what related plan material it relied on.'
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins; also 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing.'
scenarios: Agent A starts reviewing ticket T, takes the pre-LLM snapshot (no prerequisites). Agent B concurrently adds a depends_on link from T to prerequisite P. Agent A's review completes, the recheck sees T's own material is unchanged, and the attestation is signed with zero prerequisite pins — but the AC requires 'Pin coverage includes every normalized direct prerequisite.'

A prerequisite's description is edited between snapshot and sign; if the recheck only re-validates the snapshot's pin values (which were computed before the edit), the signed pin would already be stale on creation.
findings.suggested_fix: Pin collection should happen at sign time (like material and deps today), not from a pre-LLM snapshot. The 'snapshot before LLM' can record which related tickets were in scope for context assembly, but the authoritative pin fingerprints should be computed immediately before the manifest is built and signed — matching the existing pattern where material_fingerprint and dependency_hashes are computed in sign_plan_review(), not cached from the LLM call.

evidence: 30 acceptance-criteria items (> 25)
findings.finding: Oversize signals suggest this unit may be too large for one session.
findings.suggested_fix: Split into smaller child tickets, each a coherent single outcome.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a feature flag (e.g. `verify.enforce_execution_phase`, defaulting false) to gate the execution-mode 0.80 blocking floor and phase-compatibility enforcement, and extend the documented rollback path to cover this flag alongside the existing two.
evidence: 'During execution, each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.80).' — no flag governs this.
'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback' — names only the two flags, not execution-mode behavior.
'New reviews emit pins in every project regardless of enforcement configuration' — confirms at least the pin-writing change is unconditional; the execution floor similarly appears unconditional.
AC: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor' — listed as an always-on behavior, no conditional flag.
findings.finding: The execution-mode review behavior — applying the 0.80 blocking floor and enforcing phase-compatible attestation acceptance — is not behind any feature flag. This is a one-shot behavioral change that takes effect for every project the moment new code ships: any new plan review of a ticket whose reducer projection is in execution mode will immediately receive the 0.80 floor applied to its blocking criteria and must emit a `review-phase: execution` line. The two flags mentioned (`verify.enforce_plan_material_pins`, `verify.require_plan_review_for_close`) do not gate this behavior. The rollback path documented ('disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback') covers pins and close gates but leaves execution-mode review behavior unaddressed — a code rollback is therefore required to undo the execution floor, and there is no intermediate advisory/flag-only state for this feature slice.
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate; Enforcement and rollout
scenarios: Any project that has tickets currently in in_progress at the moment code ships will find that the next re-review of those tickets applies the 0.80 floor unconditionally, potentially blocking reviews that would previously have passed — with no flag to defer or disable this change while teams adapt.

A code rollback after discovering a bug in the execution-floor logic requires a full deployment rollback; there is no flag to turn off just the 0.80-floor enforcement while leaving pin behavior active, reducing operational flexibility.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce `verify.enforce_execution_phase` (default false, rebar enables it). When false, the execution-mode `review-phase` line is still written and the floor is still computed and reported, but it is advisory rather than blocking — matching the pattern used for `enforce_plan_material_pins`. Document this flag in the rollback section alongside the other two flags.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the naming collision between the plan's ticket-lifecycle 'execution mode' (planning/execution phase) and the existing prompt-level `execution_mode` (agentic/single_turn), and either choose a distinct internal name (e.g. `ticket_phase`, `review_phase`, or `lifecycle_mode`) or explicitly justify reusing the same term.
evidence: Plan: 'Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection carried in compiled state'
src/rebar/llm/prompting/prompts.py:62-74: 'execution_mode is a PROMPT-level concern: it tells the runner HOW to drive the model for this prompt — agentic … vs single_turn'
Search shows 'execution_mode' appears in 100+ source locations as agentic/single_turn enum
Plan uses 'execution mode' to mean planning/execution phase with manifest line 'review-phase: has exactly planning or execution' — orthogonal semantic domain, same vocabulary
findings.finding: The plan introduces a ticket-lifecycle 'execution mode' concept (a reducer boolean projection distinguishing planning vs. execution-phase reviews) using the same term as the firmly established prompt-level `execution_mode` field (values: agentic/single_turn) that appears in over 100 source locations across src/rebar/llm/**. The plan never acknowledges this collision or explains why the same vocabulary was chosen for an orthogonal concept. Implementors reading the plan alongside the codebase will face real ambiguity — e.g. the manifest line `review-phase: execution` vs. the existing `execution_mode: agentic` on every reviewer .md file.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: An implementor adding the reducer projection searches for 'execution_mode' in the codebase and finds only the prompt-level agentic/single_turn field, causing confusion about where to place the new field.
A code reviewer sees 'execution mode' in a PR description and confuses it with the prompt-level concept, missing a subtle architectural change.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the reducer projection to `ticket_phase` (or `review_phase`) and the manifest line key to `review-phase: planning|execution` (which the plan already uses for the manifest line). Use `ticket_phase` as the compiled-state field name to avoid any collision with the existing `execution_mode` prompt attribute.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the bootstrap rule for the execution-mode projection when reading compaction snapshots that predate the field (e.g., default false with a documented rationale, or one-time scan-from-history on first missing-field read), and confirm this rule is idempotent and safe for already-in-progress tickets.
evidence: 'Reviews read this O(1) projection, never scan raw history, so compacted tickets behave identically.'
ABSENCE: no statement about what value the projection takes when an existing compaction snapshot lacks the field.
ABSENCE: no description of a bootstrap or migration path for snapshots created before this feature.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert how existing compaction snapshots — which predate the execution-mode projection field — are handled on first post-upgrade read. The plan states 'Reviews read this O(1) projection, never scan raw history, so compacted tickets behave identically,' but if the projection field is absent from old snapshots, code must either reconstruct it from pre-compaction history (violating the O(1) claim) or silently default it to false. Neither path is specified, leaving a gap where tickets that were in_progress at compaction time could be misclassified as planning-mode after upgrade.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate: 'Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots'
scenarios: A ticket compacted while in_progress (execution mode = true) is read post-upgrade; the snapshot has no execution-mode field; code defaults it to false; an execution-phase review is now accepted as a planning-phase review, or a valid execution attestation is rejected as phase-incompatible.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single sentence to the Settled design: 'A compaction snapshot missing the execution-mode field is treated as false (planning) on read; tickets known to be in_progress at compaction time are unaffected because their next status-change event re-establishes the correct projection.' If that default is incorrect, define the correct fallback and document any one-time reconstruction step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to acaf (or 75de) requiring that relationship/pin data is rechecked immediately before signing and that a concurrent material change between snapshot and sign causes signing to fail with remediation.
evidence: Parent settled design: 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing.'

5967 Implementation: 'Snapshot lifecycle phase and material before review, then recheck both immediately before signing so concurrent transitions or edits cannot certify the wrong mode.' — covers phase and material only.

acaf Implementation: 'Make new-review collection all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate-after-normalization, or unreadable required targets prevent signing' — covers collection time only, not a post-LLM recheck.
No child AC requires verifying that pin data is rechecked under lock before the final signature when relationships change concurrently during the LLM call.
findings.finding: No child explicitly owns the sign-time recheck of pin/relationship data before signing (race-condition gap). The parent requires 'Review snapshots phase, material, and pins before the LLM call and rechecks them before signing.' Child 5967 covers sign-time recheck for phase and material, but the recheck of relationship pins (child/prerequisite drift between snapshot and signing) is not assigned to any child's AC.
findings.location: child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d — AC and Implementation; child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c — Implementation
scenarios: A child ticket's material changes concurrently between the pin snapshot (pre-LLM) and signing; the signed attestation contains a stale pin fingerprint that doesn't reflect the current child material, making the attestation immediately drifted.
A new child is added to the ticket during the LLM review call; the signed pins do not include it, violating 'Pin coverage includes every direct child.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to acaf's AC or 75de's AC: 'A concurrent relationship or material change to a pinned target between the review snapshot and signing causes the sign step to abort with an actionable error, and concurrent-sign tests cover this race.' Mirror the sign-time recheck pattern 5967 applies for phase/material.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a depends_on from 75de-02b0-d454-410b to 5967-4476-7f9f-442d (or restructure so the phase-format definition is in acaf), and explicitly note that phase-compatibility checks in compute_plan_review_validity are only active after 5967's manifest additions are integrated.
evidence: 75de Implementation: 'Own one compute_plan_review_validity profile boundary...The close profile sets check_code_freshness=false while retaining signature, registry, phase, own-material, and configured pin checks.'
75de deps: only depends_on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c.
5967 defines: 'review-phase: planning|execution' manifest encoding; 'priority-floor: 0.80'; phase-compatibility rules (planning rejects execution, execution accepts planning or execution ≥ 0.80).
5967 deps: depends_on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c AND 75de-02b0-d454-410b.
Build ordering: acaf → 75de → 5967. So 75de must implement phase checks before 5967 defines the phase format.
findings.finding: Dependency gap: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b references phase-compatibility checks in compute_plan_review_validity (e.g., 'retain...phase...checks' in the close profile) but does not depend on child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, which is the story that defines the review-phase manifest encoding. Meanwhile, 5967 depends_on 75de, making 75de implement phase checks before the phase format is defined.
findings.location: child 75de-02b0-d454-410b — Implementation; child 5967-4476-7f9f-442d — deps
scenarios: 75de implements compute_plan_review_validity with a phase check calling manifest phase parsing that hasn't been added to manifest.py yet (added by 5967), causing compile or runtime errors.
75de stubs out phase logic, leaving an incomplete validity profile that 2bbd calls before 5967 fills it in — the close gate incorrectly ignores phase compatibility during the integration window.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) move the review-phase manifest line definition (parse/emit) from 5967 into acaf so that both 75de and 5967 can depend on it, or (b) add a depends_on edge from 75de to 5967 and invert the dependency by extracting the phase-compatibility interface into a separate small story that 75de can depend on. Option (a) is cleaner: acaf already owns manifest encoding for pins; adding phase-line encoding there removes the circular logical reference.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the derived health contract schema (field names, value set, and format) that all five surfaces (CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, web) must emit, so AC #27 is verifiable against a concrete reference.
evidence: Health section states 'Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI' and 'When enforcement is off, the UI labels drift advisory' — but no field names, formats, or schema are given.
AC says 'expose the same derived health contract' — the contract itself is never enumerated (e.g., pin_status: drifted|current|advisory, review_phase: planning|execution, etc.).
ABSENCE: no example output, no named fields, no schema section exists for the health contract.
findings.finding: The 'derived health contract' is referenced in AC #27 and the Health section but never formally defined. An executor implementing display across CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, and web surfaces has no specified field names, value representations, or schema to match against — 'same derived health contract' is unverifiable without this definition.
findings.location: AC line: 'Detailed CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, and web surfaces expose the same derived health contract.' / Settled Design § Health and documentation
scenarios: An executor building the CLI `show` output and the MCP detail response cannot know which fields to include or what values to emit for the drift/advisory/valid states.
Two engineers implementing different surfaces independently will produce different schemas, causing the AC to fail even though both followed the spec.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Health contract' subsection to Settled Design listing the required fields, e.g.: `pin_drift: none | advisory | blocking`, `review_phase: planning | execution`, `floor_met: true | false | n/a`, `enforcement: enabled | disabled`. Then AC #27 becomes: 'Each of the five surfaces emits exactly these fields with these value semantics.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the expected concurrency outcome in Settled Design (e.g., 'the second writer under the store write lock receives a conflict error and must re-read before retrying; no silent data loss; an audit record is written for the losing attempt') so AC #30's concurrency scenario has a verifiable pass condition.
evidence: Settled Design states: 'Close prechecks before completion verification and rechecks under the store write lock before writing STATUS' — but does not define the observable outcome for the losing writer.
AC #30 lists 'concurrency' alongside well-defined behaviors (toggling, legacy compatibility, etc.) without any further elaboration.
ABSENCE: no section describes concurrent-close or concurrent-sign behavior (error type, retry policy, audit record).
findings.finding: AC #30 mandates 'concurrency' as a required test scenario but the plan specifies no concurrency contract — no expected outcome when two agents concurrently attempt close, sign, or re-review on the same ticket. The write-lock mention in the Settled Design is the only signal, but it does not define what the second writer observes (conflict error, retry, stale-read, etc.).
findings.location: AC line: 'Public lifecycle tests prove toggling, legacy compatibility, natural transitivity, deletion, archive handling, execution adaptation, concurrency, and force-close behavior.'
scenarios: An executor writing the concurrency test fixture cannot determine whether to assert a conflict error, a silent retry, an audit log entry, or something else — the test's pass/fail condition is undefined.
Two different implementers write conflicting assumptions into the test and the system, resulting in a test that passes for the wrong reason.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion covering context-limit retry behavior: e.g., 'When a batched prerequisite evaluation exceeds context limits, the system retries with smaller bins, and a subject-plus-one-oversized-prerequisite case triggers the existing too-large remediation rather than a silent skip.'
evidence: 'Context-limit failures retry with smaller bins. Subject plus one oversized prerequisite yields the existing too-large remediation.'
No AC verifies that context-limit retries occur or that a single oversized prerequisite triggers the existing too-large remediation path rather than a silent failure or dropped prerequisite.
findings.finding: The 'context-limit failures retry with smaller bins' behavior in the prerequisite consistency review is a described correctness path but has no covering acceptance criterion. If this retry logic is absent or wrong, the plan provides no way to detect the regression.
findings.location: Settled design — 'Prerequisite consistency review' section; Acceptance Criteria (no covering AC)
scenarios: A large prerequisite ticket causes the batched call to exceed context limits. Without a covering AC, an implementation that silently drops the prerequisite or returns an error instead of retrying with one-per-call would pass all tests.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit statement that compaction snapshots are forward-and-backward-compatible with the execution_mode field (e.g., field is optional/ignored by old readers), or add a snapshot-format versioning / migration step to the rollback plan.
evidence: 'Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots'
'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten'
ABSENCE: no mention of snapshot-format versioning, field-ignorability, or what happens when pre-feature code reads a snapshot carrying execution_mode
findings.finding: The rollback plan addresses enforcement-flag toggling but does not address compaction-snapshot format evolution: the new execution_mode boolean is carried in compiled state AND compaction snapshots, so a code rollback after snapshots have been written with the new field leaves old code reading snapshots that contain an unknown field. No expand-contract sequencing or snapshot-format versioning is described for this case.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection carried in compiled state and compaction snapshots'; Enforcement and rollout — 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback'
scenarios: Team ships the feature, compaction runs, snapshots now carry execution_mode; a rollback of the code binary then reads those snapshots with old code — if old code is strict about unknown fields it crashes or silently misreads compiled state, defeating the claimed zero-rewrite rollback.
A mixed-version deployment (e.g., two workers on different code versions) reads the same snapshot with different field sets.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence under Enforcement and rollout: 'The execution_mode field in compaction snapshots is treated as an optional boolean defaulting to false; pre-feature readers that do not recognize the field must ignore it safely. This is verified by a snapshot round-trip test with the previous schema.' This makes the rollback guarantee complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the documentation scope statement to cover review-phase and priority-floor manifest lines (same advanced attestation docs) and the two new config flags (operator/configuration reference), and explicitly note that existing manifest-format reference docs must be updated to include all three new signed lines.
evidence: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation'
ABSENCE: review-phase and priority-floor manifest lines are protocol-level fields readable by any compliant reader but have no stated documentation home
ABSENCE: verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close are operator-facing config flags with no stated documentation home
ABSENCE: no mention of updating existing attestation/manifest-format reference docs that would now be incomplete
findings.finding: The documentation scope statement only explicitly names plan-material-pin for advanced attestation docs, leaving review-phase, priority-floor manifest lines, and the two new config flags (verify.enforce_plan_material_pins, verify.require_plan_review_for_close) without a stated documentation home. Existing manifest-format documentation will be stale once the new signed lines are deployed.
findings.location: Settled design / Health and documentation — 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation'; Enforcement and rollout (config flags named but no doc home stated)
scenarios: An operator enabling the feature cannot find the semantics of review-phase or priority-floor in any docs and misconfigures enforcement.
A third-party tool implementing the manifest reader misses the new fields because the format reference doc was not updated.
A new team member reads existing manifest-format docs, does not see pin/phase/floor lines, and concludes the system is behaving incorrectly when it emits them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a measurement plan or pilot (e.g., replay a sample of historical reviews against the 0.80 floor and record false-block/false-pass rates) to empirically justify the threshold before it becomes fixed protocol policy.
evidence: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
No spike, benchmark, or pilot-with-metrics is mentioned for this threshold anywhere in the plan.
AC item: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor' — asserted as a pass criterion without a validation baseline.
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-mode blocking floor is explicitly stated as 'not an empirically claimed optimum' and 'not project config,' yet no spike, pilot, or measurement plan is described to validate that this threshold produces acceptable false-block and false-pass rates on real in-progress work.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
scenarios: The floor is too high: legitimate execution-phase reviews (e.g., those with a structural finding at 0.79) are blocked, creating friction on already-started work.
The floor is too low: borderline reviews on in-progress tickets slip through, undermining the purpose of the execution gate.
Future policy raises the floor — older attestations require re-review (the plan covers this case), but there is no data supporting whether the original 0.80 was correct to begin with.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fixture-based probe: collect ≥20 representative historical plan-review outputs, annotate expected block/pass outcomes, and verify that 0.80 produces acceptable precision/recall. Record the result as the rationale baseline. If the threshold survives, it is now evidence-backed; if not, adjust before locking it in.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a planned ADR or AGENTS.md / design-doc update entry capturing the rationale for (a) natural vs. eager propagation, (b) execution mode as a reducer projection, and (c) two-pass prerequisite attribution gating.
evidence: The 'advanced attestation/gate documentation' mention is user-facing, not architectural-decision capture.
No ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update is listed anywhere in the plan or its Not-in-scope.
Three independent architectural choices (propagation model, reducer projection, two-pass attribution pipeline) are each justified inline in the plan but have no durable decision record.
findings.finding: The plan introduces at least three novel architectural decisions — (1) signed plan-material pins with natural (non-eager) propagation, (2) execution mode as a reducer-maintained boolean projection surviving compaction, and (3) a two-pass prerequisite consistency criterion with attribution gating — but names no ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to capture the rationale for future maintainers.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation: 'plan-material-pin is documented in advanced attestation/gate documentation, not basic user documentation.'
scenarios: A future engineer modifying the attestation pipeline does not understand why natural propagation was chosen over eager invalidation, and re-introduces a write-amplification approach.
The execution-mode reducer projection is 'optimized away' during a compaction refactor because there is no doc explaining its invariant contract.
The two-pass attribution gate is loosened because the rationale (preventing cross-ID findings from blocking) is not captured outside this ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Create a single ADR (e.g., docs/adr/NNNN-plan-review-pins-and-execution-mode.md) covering all three decisions, and add a line to AGENTS.md pointing to it. Reference the ADR from the plan's 'Health and documentation' section so it becomes a tracked deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the close-profile validity check is implemented as a parameterized variant of the existing compute_validity() (e.g., skip_code_freshness=True) rather than a new parallel function, to keep the shared reopen-invalidation, material-fingerprint, and pin-drift logic in a single implementation.
evidence: Plan text: 'Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins, and other non-code attestation validity, but intentionally ignores file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD freshness because implementation normally changes impacted files.'

src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py compute_validity() (lines 471-627) is a single function handling all validity cases; the close gate currently calls this same function (transition_close.py line 323+ uses current_material_fingerprint, not a separate validity path).
ABSENCE: the plan does not state whether a 'close-profile' validity check is a new flag/parameter on compute_validity() or a new standalone function.
findings.finding: The close gate introduces a 'close-profile' attestation validity check that intentionally diverges from the existing compute_validity() by skipping file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD freshness. The plan does not specify whether this is a parameterized extension of compute_validity() (e.g., a skip_code_freshness flag) or a new parallel implementation. Two separate implementations of 'attestation validity' with slightly different semantics create a maintenance risk: a fix to one path (e.g., the reopen-invalidation logic at compute_validity line 512) may not be applied to the other, causing the close gate to silently diverge from the claim gate's behavior on edge cases like the reopen-then-close sequence.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate: 'Close checks signature, compatible phase, the ticket's own material, relationship pins, and other non-code attestation validity, but intentionally ignores file dependency hashes and whole-HEAD freshness'
scenarios: A ticket is reopened, then a plan review is signed (post-reopen), then closed. The reopen-invalidation check (compute_validity lines 511-517) must apply to the close-profile check too. If close-profile is a separate implementation, it might omit this check, allowing a pre-reopen attestation to pass the close gate.
A future fix to compute_validity's stale-material path is not mirrored to the close-profile path, causing the close gate to accept a materially-stale attestation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a skip_code_freshness: bool = False parameter to compute_validity(). The close gate passes skip_code_freshness=True, skipping the dep-hash and whole-HEAD checks but retaining reopen-invalidation, material fingerprint, phase compatibility, and pin drift checks — all in one implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Provide a prior-art or calibration basis for the 0.80 execution-review blocking floor — either cite a reference to comparable phase-aware review threshold practices, apply the existing impact-distribution methodology (scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py) to derive an evidence-backed value, or document why the existing novelty/completion floor calibration methodology does not apply here.
evidence: Plan: 'The 0.80 value is a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config.'
src/rebar/_config_schema.py:271-275: novelty_priority_floor=0.4 documented as 'scalar at the corpus p40 impact percentile (~0.4, the below major band; see scripts/plan_review_impact_distribution.py)'
src/rebar/_config_schema.py:281-298: completion_priority_floor=0.4 with 'calibrated before flip: gold-set 30/30 floor decisions correct, κ=1.0' (in rebar.toml comment)
ABSENCE: no reference in the plan to any analogous phase-aware threshold in the existing codebase or external systems; no pilot data cited for 0.80
findings.finding: The 0.80 execution-review blocking floor is declared as 'a fixed protocol risk policy for already-started work, not an empirically claimed optimum or project config,' yet the plan provides no prior-art reference, no calibration basis, and no comparison to established approaches for phase-aware review thresholds. The existing codebase documents its priority floors with calibration evidence (novelty_priority_floor=0.4 grounded at corpus p40 impact percentile with a calibration script; completion_priority_floor=0.4 matched to it with a 30/30 gold-set). The plan introduces a higher floor (0.80) for execution-mode reviews with no analogous justification — no pilot data, no reference to how comparable systems (e.g., code-review risk escalation under feature-freeze, or the existing Pass-3 novelty/completion floors) handle already-started work, and no explanation of why 0.80 rather than, say, 0.70 (the existing novelty_drop_threshold) or a value derived from existing calibration.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate; AC: 'Execution reviews apply the fixed 0.80 blocking floor'
scenarios: The 0.80 floor blocks advisory findings that would normally be visible, causing legitimate execution-phase issues to be silently suppressed or surfaced without rationale.
A future policy change requires raising the floor to 0.85; without a calibration basis there is no principled way to evaluate the change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert, in the rollback section, that compaction snapshots are forward-compatible with pre-feature code (i.e., old readers ignore unknown snapshot fields), or add an explicit DATA LOSS RISK note and a compensating-forward remediation step (e.g., 'before rollback, trigger re-compaction without the execution-mode field for all affected tickets').
evidence: Plan explicitly handles manifest backward-compat ('Readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines') but makes no equivalent statement for compaction snapshots.
ABSENCE: no assertion that old compaction-snapshot readers gracefully ignore unknown fields, and no DATA LOSS RISK or remediation step for affected snapshots.
findings.finding: The plan's rollback description covers only attestation manifests ('no stored attestation must be rewritten') but does not assert the schema state of compaction snapshots after a code rollback. If old code cannot tolerate an unknown execution-mode field in a compaction snapshot, rolling back the code while snapshots carrying the new field already exist will leave those tickets unreadable until their snapshots are manually repaired.
findings.location: Settled design — Enforcement and rollout: 'Operational rollback is disabling both enforcement flags before or with a code rollback; no stored attestation must be rewritten.'
scenarios: Code is rolled back after several tickets have been compacted with the execution-mode field present; old code chokes on the unknown field; those tickets are inaccessible until snapshots are manually deleted/recompacted.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact insertion position of `review-phase:` and `priority-floor:` lines within `build_manifest()`'s output sequence, and specify the corresponding parser functions (e.g. `manifest_review_phase()`, `manifest_priority_floor()`) alongside their validation rules.
evidence: Plan text: 'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80.'

Existing build_manifest() in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/manifest.py produces a fixed ordered line list with parsers: manifest_material(), manifest_regver(), manifest_deps(), manifest_disabled_builtins() — no parser for review-phase or priority-floor is defined anywhere in the plan.
Plan never specifies where in the line sequence review-phase: and priority-floor: are inserted, though position matters for is_plan_review_manifest() which tests manifest[0].
findings.finding: Two new manifest line types (`review-phase:` and `priority-floor:`) are referenced throughout the plan but their insertion position within `build_manifest()`'s deterministic line order, and the corresponding parser functions analogous to `manifest_material()` / `manifest_regver()`, are never specified. Executors must know exact line ordering (e.g. `is_plan_review_manifest` relies on `manifest[0]`) and must have named reader functions to check compatibility at claim, close, and audit time.
findings.location: Settled Design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'The signed manifest line review-phase: has exactly planning or execution. Execution reviews also carry priority-floor: 0.80.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the close gate phase-compatibility check needs to call a reader like `manifest_review_phase(manifest)` — undefined in the plan.
An executor placing the new lines in `build_manifest()` has no spec for their ordinal position, risking non-deterministic manifests across implementations.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Manifest line contract' sub-section to the Settled Design that lists every new line prefix, its position (e.g., immediately after `material:`), its parser name, and its validation predicate. Confirm that legacy readers ignoring unknown lines remain safe regardless of insertion position.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a `manifest_pins()` parser function (returning a list of `{role, canonical_id, fingerprint}` records) and define the error-handling protocol for unknown roles, malformed fields, and conflicting duplicates — including whether they surface as `indeterminate` or as hard validation errors that invalidate the attestation.
evidence: Plan text: 'New plan reviews encode each pin as the signed manifest line plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>. The only role strings are child and prerequisite. Lines sort by role then canonical ID.'

Plan text: 'Unknown roles, malformed fields, or conflicting duplicate values are invalid.' — stated as a rule but the error-handling protocol (what the enforcement evaluator does with invalid lines) is unspecified.
No manifest_pins() or equivalent parser is defined; the existing manifest.py family (manifest_deps, manifest_material, manifest_regver) is the pattern to follow but no analogous function is named.
findings.finding: The `plan-material-pin:` manifest line format is introduced but no parser function or parse-protocol is specified for reading pins back out of a manifest during enforcement checks. The plan states validation rules ('unknown roles, malformed fields, or conflicting duplicate values are invalid') but never co-locates those rules with a defined parser contract analogous to the existing `manifest_deps()` / `manifest_material()` family.
findings.location: Settled Design / Signed plan-material pins — 'New plan reviews encode each pin as the signed manifest line plan-material-pin: <role> <canonical-ticket-id> <material-fingerprint>.'
scenarios: An executor implementing pin-drift enforcement at claim/close time needs to parse all `plan-material-pin:` lines from the signed manifest — the parsing contract (field delimiter, field count validation, what happens on a malformed line) is absent.
A 'conflicting duplicate' pin line (same role + canonical ID but different fingerprint) must be detected; the detection algorithm is referenced but not specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the complete schema of the prerequisite coverage record, including what field (and with what value vocabulary) captures the reason for an `indeterminate` disposition, so implementers and test-fixture authors have a single authoritative contract.
evidence: Plan text: '{prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}, where disposition is consistent, finding, or indeterminate. consistent requires an empty findings list; finding requires one or more candidate findings all attributed to that same prerequisite_id; indeterminate records why evaluation or attribution could not be completed'
The schema specifies fields `prerequisite_id`, `disposition`, `findings` but the content of `findings` on an `indeterminate` record and the 'why' capture field are absent.

Existing coverage records in the codebase (e.g., plan_review_verdict.schema.json) use `resolution_class` enums for similar 'why' capture — no analogous vocabulary is defined for prerequisite indeterminate reasons.
findings.finding: The prerequisite coverage record's `indeterminate` disposition includes a `records why evaluation or attribution could not be completed` obligation, but the sub-structure of the 'why' field (a free-text string, a structured reason code, or a set of named reasons like 'context_limit | unresolvable_id | attribution_failure') is never defined, leaving the Pass-2 verifier and the indeterminate-detection path with an under-specified output schema.
findings.location: Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review — 'Pass 1 returns exactly one coverage record per canonical prerequisite: {prerequisite_id, disposition, findings}, where disposition is consistent, finding, or indeterminate. ... indeterminate records why evaluation or attribution could not be completed and cannot block.'
scenarios: An executor logging why a prerequisite evaluation was indeterminate (e.g., for audit output) has no field to write to.
The prerequisite-packing release gate requires 'every prerequisite must receive a record' — if the indeterminate structure is unspecified, test fixture construction is ambiguous.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `reason` field to the indeterminate record with a closed vocabulary (e.g., `context_limit | unresolvable_id | attribution_failure | llm_degraded | schema_invalid`) mirroring the existing `resolution_class` pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the Pass-2 output contract for the prerequisite consistency criterion, specifying whether attribution verification is a new `GRADED_BINARY` sub-answer (e.g., `attribution_matches_prerequisite_id`), a separate structured field, or a deterministic post-Pass-2 filter — and provide the decision rule for 'upheld' vs. 'unattributed/cross-ID'.
evidence: Plan text: 'Pass 2 receives the same subject/prerequisite map and verifies substance and prerequisite_id attribution.'

Existing GRADED_BINARY in src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py: ('is_verifiable', 'evidence_entails_finding', 'path_reachable', 'impact_follows_necessarily', 'no_viable_alternative_explanation', 'no_existing_mitigation', 'severity_claim_justified', 'committed_work_relies_on_unbacked_claim', 'respects_artifact_altitude', 'asserted_capability_confirmed') — no attribution sub-answer present.
The plan says attribution verification is a Pass-2 concern but gives no new sub-answer name, no structured output field, and no decision rule for 'upheld vs. unattributed'.
findings.finding: Pass 2 for the prerequisite consistency criterion must verify both substance AND `prerequisite_id` attribution, but no verifier schema extension or new sub-answer is defined for the attribution check. The existing `GRADED_BINARY` sub-answer set contains no sub-answer for cross-prerequisite attribution, yet the plan states 'unattributed or cross-ID findings cannot block' — implying a new binary decision is needed but its schema is absent.
findings.location: Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review — 'Pass 2 receives the same subject/prerequisite map and verifies substance and prerequisite_id attribution. Only an upheld finding record can proceed to normal Pass 3 and the execution floor; unattributed or cross-ID findings cannot block.'
scenarios: An executor implementing Pass-2 for prerequisite findings must produce an 'attributed to prerequisite_id X' verdict — with no defined sub-answer, they cannot use the existing validity() arithmetic to reach that verdict.
A finding that mentions two prerequisites might be upheld on substance but flagged cross-ID — the decision rule for this case is absent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define 'resolved transition' precisely (e.g., 'any STATUS event successfully written to the store, including force-close') and enumerate any exclusions, so the reducer projection rule is unambiguous for all transition types.
evidence: Plan text: 'Every resolved transition into in_progress sets it true. Every resolved transition into open clears it. Other statuses preserve it.'

'Resolved transition' is used as a technical term but is not defined in the plan; the existing ticket-state model has concepts like rejected transitions, blocked transitions, force-transitions — which of these are 'resolved'?

The plan's example 'open -> in_progress -> blocked remains execution; blocked -> open resets planning' implies 'resolved' means 'the transition event was committed/written', but this is inferred rather than stated.
findings.finding: The term 'resolved transition' used to define execution-mode state-machine transitions ('Every resolved transition into in_progress sets it true. Every resolved transition into open clears it.') is not defined anywhere in the plan, yet the reducer implementation must know precisely which transition events increment or clear the projection — an ambiguous decision-rule placement.
findings.location: Settled Design / Execution reviews and close gate — 'Execution mode is an internal reducer-maintained boolean projection... Every resolved transition into in_progress sets it true. Every resolved transition into open clears it.'
scenarios: A force-close or rollback transition back to open — does this count as a 'resolved' transition that clears execution mode, or is it excluded?
A rejected status-change attempt — the reducer must know whether to update the projection on an attempted-but-rejected transition.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'resolved transition' with a reference to the specific event type (e.g., 'any committed STATUS event whose target status is in_progress / open') and explicitly state whether force-transitions are included.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the derived health contract: enumerate the named health states (e.g., `valid`, `pin-drift-advisory`, `pin-drift-invalid`, `phase-incompatible`, `stale-pin`), the conditions that produce each state, and the label string each surface must render — so AC #27 is testable.
evidence: Plan text: 'Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI. Ordinary list output remains unchanged. Health is derived at read time; no warning event is persisted. When enforcement is off, the UI labels drift advisory.'
AC #27: 'Detailed CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, and web surfaces expose the same derived health contract.'

The health-state vocabulary (what values can each surface display, and under what conditions) is absent — e.g., is there a 'stale-pin' state distinct from 'pin-drift'? Is 'phase-mismatch' a separate label?
Prior cache review 4396f0bdfd05f634d99f.json also flagged this absence.
findings.finding: The 'derived health contract' referenced in both the Health section and AC #27 ('Detailed CLI, MCP, audit, close-error, and web surfaces expose the same derived health contract') is never formally enumerated: no fields, labels, or condition-to-label mappings are defined. The plan says the UI 'labels drift advisory' when enforcement is off, but no vocabulary of health states (e.g., 'valid', 'pin-drift-advisory', 'pin-drift-invalid', 'phase-mismatch', 'stale-pin') is provided for the five named surfaces to implement consistently.
findings.location: Settled Design / Health and documentation — 'Derived pin and review-phase health appears in detailed CLI show, MCP ticket detail, audit output, close errors, and the web ticket-review UI.'
scenarios: An executor implementing the CLI `show` command for pin health has no enumerated states to render, leading to inconsistent labels across CLI/MCP/audit surfaces.
AC #27 cannot be tested without a defined shared contract — there is no ground truth to assert against.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the context-isolation mechanism: specify that the compiled prerequisite state map is used ONLY for canonical ID resolution and pin collection (never injected into general-reviewer LLM call inputs), and that the prerequisite-consistency criterion runs as a strictly separate LLM call with its own isolated context.
evidence: Plan text: 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General reviewers do not receive prerequisite material.'

Plan text: 'The review snapshot preloads compiled ticket states once and reuses that map for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery, avoiding a separate full-store reduction for every prerequisite.'

The plan does not specify whether general-reviewer LLM calls share a context window with prerequisite material or receive strictly separate inputs — the 'reuses that map' phrasing is ambiguous about context-window sharing.
findings.finding: The plan loads prerequisite ticket material into the review context for the prerequisite-consistency criterion while also stating 'General reviewers do not receive prerequisite material.' This creates an instruction-locality / pink-elephant risk: the plan does not specify how prerequisite material is isolated from the general finder prompts (e.g., whether prerequisites are loaded in a separate call with no cross-contamination, or whether a single shared context is used with a negative instruction). The 'Review snapshot preloads compiled ticket states once and reuses that map' description suggests shared state, but the isolation mechanism is unspecified.
findings.location: Settled Design / Prerequisite consistency review — 'A specialized criterion runs deterministically when direct prerequisites exist. General reviewers do not receive prerequisite material. The review snapshot preloads compiled ticket states once and reuses that map for canonical resolution plus inbound blocks discovery.'
scenarios: If compiled prerequisite states are passed as part of the shared context to general finder LLM calls, those reviewers see prerequisite material despite the stated restriction, causing pink-elephant violations where reviewers comment on prerequisites they were instructed to ignore.
An executor interpreting 'reuses that map' as a single shared prompt context will violate the isolation requirement.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: 'The compiled prerequisite state map is used only for canonical ID resolution, inbound-blocks discovery, and pin fingerprint collection. It is never included in the inputs passed to general finder or verifier LLM calls.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC and test in acaf (or cb6b) verifying that a pre-feature manifest parser silently skips unknown plan-material-pin lines when reading a new-format manifest.
evidence: Parent AC8: 'Readers predating the feature safely ignore additive pin lines.'
Parent settled design: 'Pin lines are additive manifest metadata: readers predating this feature ignore unknown lines and therefore treat the attestation like a legacy unpinned review.'
acaf AC3: 'legacy unpinned manifests still parse' — covers NEW reader, OLD manifest only.
acaf Implementation: 'Preserve byte-compatible parsing of legacy manifests that have no pins' — same direction.
No child AC explicitly tests or specifies an old-code reader handling a new manifest containing plan-material-pin lines.
findings.finding: AC8 ('Readers predating the feature safely ignore additive pin lines') is only partially covered: no child has an AC or test plan verifying that OLD readers safely ignore plan-material-pin lines in NEW-format manifests. Child acaf covers only the reverse direction (new readers parsing old manifests without pins), not pre-feature readers encountering new manifests.
findings.location: child acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c — AC3 and Implementation
scenarios: A pre-feature rebar version reads a manifest written by the new version; if pin lines are not silently ignored, attestation verification fails or raises errors in existing deployments.
Operational rollback scenario: team rolls back code but new-format attestations exist in the store; pre-rollback readers must safely skip pin lines.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend acaf's AC3 to add: 'A manifest parser predating pin support silently ignores plan-material-pin lines and produces the same validity result as a legacy-unpinned attestation.' Add a corresponding unit test using a simulated old parser (or parameterized test) that reads a new-format manifest and confirms no error or invalid outcome.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a depends_on from 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 to 5967-4476-7f9f-442d so that the execution-phase floor infrastructure is guaranteed available when prerequisite finding routing is implemented.
evidence: 7c27 Implementation: 'Route upheld findings through normal Pass 3 and the execution-phase floor.'
7c27 deps: depends_on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c only.
5967 defines: execution review applies 'effective blocking threshold max(normal criterion threshold, 0.8)'; this is the floor routing that Pass 3 must use.
5967 deps: depends_on acaf and 75de — no edge to or from 7c27.
No stated ordering between 7c27 and 5967 in the dependency graph.
findings.finding: Dependency gap: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 routes upheld prerequisite findings 'through normal Pass 3 and the execution-phase floor' but only depends_on acaf-bb4d-9eba-470c. The execution-phase floor (0.80 blocking threshold, floor application logic) is defined by 5967-4476-7f9f-442d, which has no dependency relationship with 7c27. 7c27 could be implemented before or in parallel with 5967, leaving the floor routing unimplemented.
findings.location: child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56 — Implementation and deps
scenarios: 7c27 is implemented before 5967; prerequisite findings route through Pass 3 but the execution-floor logic doesn't exist yet, so execution-mode reviews do not apply the 0.80 floor to prerequisite findings.
At integration, prerequisite findings bypass the 0.80 floor silently because the floor-application code path is not wired into Pass 3's prerequisite routing.
findings.suggested_fix: Add depends_on: 5967-4476-7f9f-442d to child 7c27-10fc-4fe6-4b56. The prerequisite criterion story should explicitly state that Pass-3 floor routing reuses the existing threshold infrastructure added by 5967, and this dependency ensures sequencing.

findings.finding: The plan says execution mode is 'derived from STATUS event history' (specifically: 'the ticket is executing iff it has entered in_progress since its most recent transition to open'), but the existing compaction mechanism folds STATUS events into a SNAPSHOT and may discard raw event files. The current compiled state accumulates `last_reopened_at` (closed→open) but has no 'has_entered_in_progress_since_last_open' flag. Post-compaction, the raw STATUS history needed to derive execution mode may be unrecoverable. The plan does not specify whether (a) a new reducer-accumulated field is added to compiled state (the robust path) or (b) a raw-event scan is performed (fragile under compaction). An implementer could choose the wrong path and break execution-mode detection for any ticket that has been compacted.
findings.location: Settled design — Execution reviews and close gate: 'Execution mode is derived from STATUS event history, not from a new ticket field or a prior attestation'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new compiled-state boolean field (e.g. `execution_mode: bool`) maintained by `process_status`: set `True` on any `*→in_progress` transition when the prior `closed→open` reopen is more recent than the last `execution_mode` reset, and reset to `False` on any `*→open` transition. This makes execution-mode O(1) at review time, survives compaction (the SNAPSHOT carries compiled state), and mirrors the existing `last_reopened_at` pattern.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
